description: Small 1/2 inch long, red, fleshy fruit ripens in fall within a red capsule. This red, pink, ivory to yellow capsule splits to show orange-red seeds in the fall that are attractive to birds. The fall berries are eaten by birds, and the seeds are easily spread by birds. An obovoid, dehiscent capsule; single seed enclosed in orange-red aril.
origin: South Siberia to East Asia, Japan and China.
synonyms: ["Euonymus arakianus", "Euonymus alatus var. verus", "Euonymus striatus var. pilosus", "Euonymus alatus subvar. pilosus", "Euonymus alatus subvar. subtriflorus", "Euonymus alatus subsp. sacrosanctus", "Euonymus alatus f. striatus", "Euonymus striatus var. cornucarpus", "Euonymus subtriflorus", "Euonymus alatus f. pilosus"]

care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness. Heart rhythm abnormalities with large doses.

care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.

description: The flowers are replaced by 4-lobed seed capsules that become mature during the fall. Each seed capsule splits open into four parts to expose fleshy red fruits) that each contain two seeds. The seed capsules are light pink or pale purple with a smooth surface; they later become more dark-colored. The seeds are light brown with a smooth surface.
origin: Eastern North America, Canada and Eastern United States.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.used_for: ["Bark", "leaves and fruits were formerly used for medicinal purposes; however", "all parts of the plant are poisonous if ingested."]
care.used_for_details: Bark, leaves and fruits were formerly used for medicinal purposes; however, all parts of the plant are poisonous if ingested.

description: Euonymus europaeus, the spindle, European spindle, or common spindle, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, native to much of Europe, where it inhabits the edges of forest, hedges and gentle slopes, tending to thrive on nutrient-rich, chalky and salt-poor soils. It is a deciduous shrub or small tree.
origin: Europe to Caucasus and western Asia.
synonyms: ["Euonymus angustifolius", "Euonymus europaeus var. australis", "Euonymus suberosus", "Euonymus vulgaris var. microphyllus", "Euonymus multiflorus", "Euonymus latifolius", "Euonymus quadrigonus", "Euonymus flavescens", "Euonymus fibrillifer", "Euonymus europaeus var. suberosus"]

care.used_for_details: The whole plant yields a volatile oil that is used in soap making. A high quality charcoal is obtained from the wood, it is used by artists.
care.edible_parts: Toxic when eaten in quantity by humans.

description: Fertile flowers are replaced by seed capsules that become mature during the autumn. Mature seed capsules are white, globoid, and about ¼ inch across. Eventually, they split open to reveal fleshy arils that are orange to red; solitary seeds occurs within these arils.
origin: East Asia, China, Japan, and Korea.
synonyms: ["Cassine fortunei", "Elaeodendron fortunei"]

common_problems: Powdery mildew can be a problem, as can mites, leaf miners, aphids, mealybugs, and crown gall.
Additional diseases which may appear include witches  broom, stem dieback, and fungal leaf spots.
It can also become weedy in disturbed areas around houses and buildings.
common_pests: It is susceptible to euonymus scale and is frequently damaged by deer.
Powdery mildew can be a problem, as can mites, leaf miners, aphids, mealybugs, and crown gall.
Plants can be susceptible to attacks by caterpillars and are often damaged during the flowering season.
care.edible_parts: Toxic if eaten in large quantities.
